Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (As lights come up, we hear the sound of music, laughter coming from Angel’s. Roy comes through the door with a bang, grinning and singing a snatch of a country song. He crosses down to the bench, gets himself a beer, sits, sips, and stares at the stars like a man who has got a grudge against them, and continues his reverie.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Starlight, starbright
Twenty-fifth star I seen tonight
I wish I may, I wished I might
Have the wish I . . .
(He sips beer.)
Starlight, starbright
Twenty-sixth star I seen tonight
I wished I may, I wished I might
Have the wish I . . .

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (He sips beer. Howls like a coyote. He points his finger like a gun. Holds it to his head. Fires. He falls over.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You all right, doc? (Pause. Gasping.)
It’s all right, Wyatt. It’s just a flesh wound.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (From offstage we hear Ray calling for his brother. He’s been running from the parking lot through Angel’s; he arrives slightly out of breath.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY (offstage)
Lines.Line: Roy! Roy!

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray enters. Sees Roy lying down.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Roy? You out here? Roy? Roy?
Is that you, Roy?

Lines.Speaker: ROY (still lying)
Lines.Line: No. It’s Dale Evans. Queen of the Goddamned West.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Have you passed out?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: No. I was just killing myself.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy sits up.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Are you drunk, Roy?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Is it Friday night, Ray?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It’s Friday night, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Then I’m drunk, Ray. (Pause.)
Did you take my wife home for me?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I did.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Good. (Pause.) How’s my 1959 pink Thunderbird convertible?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It’s good.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: That’s good. How’s it runnin’?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Rough.

Lines.Speaker: ROY (concerned)
Lines.Line: What?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Probably need your points seein’ to.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: O.K.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: And your plugs.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: New plugs?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Maybe a new radiator cap.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Un-hun.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: And your tires are low.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Un-hun.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Pause.) Actually could use a whole new block.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Drop my block?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Un-hun.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Still. She’s a helluva automobile.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You want me to drive you home?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Little while.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You drunk ain’t you, Roy?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Not much. Sorta.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I been into Angel’s.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I figured.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You wanna go back in there?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What for.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Bright lights. Good music. Conversation.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Who do I want to talk to in there?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: James is in there. Cletis is in there. We could talk to Cletis Fullenroy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I’d rather talk to dirt.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You’re gettin’ unfriendly, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I’m a mean son of a bitch, Ray.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I know it.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: War made me mean.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You were always mean.

Lines.Speaker: ROY (intensely)
Lines.Line: That goddamn Skeeter Fullenroy. I hate that little bastard.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Why?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: He wears them little stupid-ass high-school loafers. I hate ’em.
And he has that thing to hold pencils in his pockets. I’d like to kill him.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: He runs his daddy’s appliance store now.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Does that mean I can’t kill him?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: He’s on the Junior Chamber of Commerce. (Pause.) Nice night.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: It is.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: That’s for damn sure.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Look at them stars, Ray.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Any one in particular?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: No. Just the whole thing. Each star is a sun, Ray. Most of ’em as big as our sun.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Some even bigger. (Pause.) If we went in we could listen to the juke box. Got all your favorites, Roy. You could play A-5, “Your Cheatin’ Heart,” or B-6, “Return to Sender,” or C-8, “Get Offa My Cloud.”

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray begins to sing “Get Offa My Cloud” loudly.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Sit down here and have a Star, little brother, and shut up!

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray takes a Lone Star beer. Sits on bench with his brother.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Cold.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Un-huh. (Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It’s nice in there.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What do I want to go in there for. It’s just a juke joint with a lot a sluts and rednecks who want to break your nose for you.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: But that’s your kinda place, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Normally, yeah. But not tonight.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: There’s some blondes in there.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Look. I’m a married man. I don’t need strange all the time. Just once in a while. (Pause. They sip.) I like it out here.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I know you do.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: We done a lot a growin’ up around here. (Pause.) When I was in Viet Nam, I used to say to myself, “Roy.”

Lines.Speaker: RAY (giggling)
Lines.Line: You called yourself Roy?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What the hell you want me to call myself?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I mean you talked to yourself out loud?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Sometimes. War is a terrible thing, Ray. Anyway, I’d say, “Roy, if you ever get back to Maynard, Texas, you go out back to Angel’s like you and Wayne Wilder used to do.” Remember Wayne?

Lines.Speaker: RAY (grimly)
Lines.Line: How could I forget.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: 'And you get yourself a case of Lone Star beer, some peanuts, some popcorn, some Fritos, a box of Cracker Jacks maybe, some Baby Ruths, a couple of Mars Bars, and all the beef jerky and Slim Jims you can lay your hands on.''

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You got Mars Bars out here?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: 'And you just stretch out and look at the land and listen to the cars go by on the highway.'' You don’t know how many times I thought about that. I promised myself that was the first damn thing I’d do, was get drunk in back of Angel’s. And now I’m doin’ it.
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You been back two years now, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: So what? (With emphasis.) I served my time.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, you do this every night.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: But you see, when you’re trying to come back to a place in your mind you want it to be how you remember it. Not how it is. This place is still the same cause the stars never change. (Pause.) Sides I got my Lone Star, and all my little do-dads spread out here in front of me. I’m doin’ exactly what I said I’d do.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You’ve always been a man of your word, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (ROY points his finger in Ray’s chest for emphasis.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: And don’t forget: I served my time.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (RAY, ashamed.)
They wouldn’t take me. You know I had football knee.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What you had is football brain. And when you’re too stupid to get into the Army, you’re too dumb to breathe. Now that’s what I call dumb.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Not so dumb I didn’t go to Nam and get myself shot.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What did you just call it?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Nam. That’s what you call it.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Don’t try to be cool. You can’t say Nam. You weren’t there. It’s Viet Nam to you.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Viet Nam then. I didn’t get my ass shot off in Viet Nam. That’s for damn sure.
(ROY points finger.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: All I know is: I served my time.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You been out to the house lately?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What for. Mom’s gone senile and the Old Man’s a damn fool anyway.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Why you say our Father’s a fool?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Warned us Roy and Ray didn’t he.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray begins to rummage in the paper sacks.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What you lookin’ for?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: They Mars Bar.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: When are you gonna learn to talk?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I talk fine.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You talk for shit. It ain’t: ''They Mars Bar.'' It’s ''this Mars Bar'' or ''that Mars Bar'' or ''his Mars Bars'' or ''her Mars Bars.''

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What if she only has one.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Then it’s Mar Bar. She has one Mar Bar. He has one Mar Bar. They have one Mar Bar. (Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You got a Baby Ruth?

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy pulls one from jean jacket pocket. Ray unwraps it. Takes a bite, takes a sip of beer.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Have you ever noticed how a Baby Ruth looks a lot like a turd? I mean, it’s about the size of a turd. More or less. It’s got little bumps on it. Worse of all it’s brown.
(He takes a bite.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Sure are good though.
(He takes a sip of beer.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: That’s not the right way to do that.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Eat a Baby Ruth and drink a beer.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I been eatin Baby Ruths all my life.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Listen to me.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Baby Ruths is one thing I know everything about.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Look, there’s a right way and wrong way to do everything. That’s one thing the Army teaches you.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: The Army teaches you how to eat a Baby Ruth?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: The two tastes don’t mix.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I don’t understand.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Look. (Holding up beer.) Beer sour. (Holding up Baby Ruth.) Baby Ruth sweet. Now you get it?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Christ. You need (Holding up popcorn.) popcorn.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Popcorn?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Popcorn salty.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Show.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: First you eat the Baby Ruth.
(He takes a bite.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Mm. Nutty and delicious.
(He takes popcorn.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Then you eat the popcorn. Hm. Salty and crunchy.
(Takes beer.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Then you drink the beer. Hm. Cold and sour. Now you try.
(Ray takes bite of Baby Ruth, then the popcorn, then sips beer.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Well?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I like it better my way.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You’d have never made it in the Army.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Then I’m glad I didn’t go.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You couldn't get in.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (RAY, mouth full.)
Football knee.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You couldn't've took it for five minutes.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Maybe I could.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You get squeamish when you see a dead armadillo out on the highway.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I don't like it when their shells are cracked open.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Shit. I saw people without heads.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Were they dead?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Think about it, Ray.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I guess so.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I saw a guy stick an M-16 up a Cook's cunt and fire it.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Ooooh.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I saw guys burn babies.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I'm glad I flunked that test.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Damn right. Here I'm gonna show you what it was like.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Don't want to.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Now you sit there.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Who am I.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You're a Cook.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I knew it.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Now I'm gonna sneak up on you.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray sits eating popcorn. Roy picks up two by four. Roy removes himself a ways and flattens himself on the ground and begins crawling.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, I'll just shoot you.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You wouldn't see me.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Hell I wouldn't.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I'm bein' too stealthy.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: But I see you.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: No, you're talkin' to someone.
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Who am I talkin' to?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Anybody.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Can it be J.R.?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yes!
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: “Well look-a there J.R. You see what I see.” “I sure do, Ray. Looks like a goddamn American soldier.” “Reckon we better shoot the son-bitch.”

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: No! No! Cut! Look. You're talkin' to your friend Gung-ho. And you don't see me. Got it?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: O.K.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy resumes crawling.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, howdy, Gung. Damn long time no see. How the wife and kids? What's that you say? No. I don't understand Chinese.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: That's it. Now you see I'm sneakin' up all the time. An' you don't see me.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What's that Gung? You say you see goddamn American soldier. Reckon we better shoot the son of a bitch.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray stands with a pistol in his finger. Shoots Roy.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You're dead.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: No I'm not.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I just killed you.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You couldn't see me!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Gung saw you.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: No he didn't. Gung is blind.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Shit, I didn't know that.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You're blind too. O.K. We're gonna do it again.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy resumes crawling.)
(Ray closes his eyes simulating blindness. He feels the air for his friend ''Gung ho.'')

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well Gung, how you doin'? You still blind? Me too. Guess we're just sitting ducks if some goddamn American soldier tries to sneak up on us.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray throws a handful of popcorn into his ''blind'' face.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Good thing we surrounded ourselves with land mines.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy buries face in dirt.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Shit. What's the use?

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy gets up.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I don't like to play Viet Nam, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Silence. They drink.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Suddenly.) Where the hell is everybody!?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, Cletis and James are inside…

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I'm not talkin' 'bout them. I'm talkin' about people I knew. My friends from before. Guys I used to go up to Dallas with and pick up girls. Damn we had ourselves some times.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Most a your crowd done gone, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (ROY, nostalgically.)
There was old Palmer Jenkins.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: He moved to Oklahoma.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Don't talk to me about Oklahoma.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, that's where he moved to. Hugo, Oklahoma.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Goddamnit. I asked you. Why would anybody in their right mind move from Maynard, Texas to Hugo, Oklahoma?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I couldn't say. But he sells used Buicks there.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (ROY, deathly serious.)
That's the goddamned end of the line you know that? Sellin' used Buicks in Hugo, Oklahoma.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I wouldn't do it for money.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Goddamn. You move to Oklahoma, you know what that makes you don’t it?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: A goddamn Okie! The lowest form of life that lives!

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (This gives Roy an idea.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Say, Ray, what was the best time you ever had?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Slightest pause.)
R.O.T.C.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You liked that?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I liked them uniforms.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Well, this year we’re gonna go to Texas–O.U. weekend.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yeah?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: We’re gonna sit in the Cotton Bowl.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yeah?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: We’re gonna watch the Longhorns beat the shit out of Oklahoma and yell hook ’em horns, just like all the other assholes.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Hook ’em Horns!

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Then you know what?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: We’re gonna get a room on the tenth floor of a hotel in downtown Dallas…

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yeah?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: And throw all the furniture out the window.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Awed.)
Man.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: How does that sound?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: We’ll have ourselves a time!

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Damn straight.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yeeow!

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Then we’ll get ourselves a forty dollar whore.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Huh?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: And go to hell in a wheelbarrow!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You mean a prostitute?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Don’t you like the idea?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, sure…

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Say so if you don’t.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, I just…

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You haven’t had that much experience with women is what you tryin’ to say.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Pause.)
I know more than Cletis knows.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What does Cletis know?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Hardly nothing at all.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I’m not surprised.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: But he’s been married two years.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Some girl married Skeeter Fullenroy.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Confidentially.)
He’d never seen a fully grown naked woman before.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: He’s been married two years and he’s still never seen a naked woman?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Not before he got married. He’d never seen a naked woman before he got married. But he had seen a book.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: A book?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yes. It showed the female reproductive organs.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Well, that should have given him an idea.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No. It just showed a side view.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Uh-huh.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: So I told him what I knew.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: About what?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: About naked women.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Chuckling.)
Uh-huh.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I’ve had some experience, Roy. I have.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Pause.)
Where the hell is everybody? Where the hell is Dell Henry?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: He got married, moved to Snyder.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I know, I’m just talkin out loud.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Darkly.)
Then there’s old Wayne.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (With great fondness.)
Old Wayne. We won’t see his like again. Biggest hell raiser this town ever saw.
(Pause.)
Guess he’s still down to Huntsville.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Been out two years. Good behavior.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Now, Wayne was always smart. Remember, he got out of the Army by shootin off his toe.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Blew off half his foot.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Old Wayne.
(Pause.)
Shit. Everyone I knew is either married, moved to Oklahoma, or shot their foot off, and I can’t decide which is worse.
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Darkly.)
Wayne was a mean bastard.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Appalled.)
What makes you say a thing like that?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: He hit me on the head with a hoe.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Pause.)
He was probably trying to teach you something. Probably had a moral to it.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What kind of moral can there be when somebody hits you on the head with a hoe?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Did I ever tell you about the time ol’ Wayne and me went to Bossier City, Louisiana?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: About a hundred times.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Shouting as if in an echo canyon.)

Bossier City! Bossier City! Kinda got a sound to it, don’t it? Bossier City! Babylon on the Red River! Sin. Hot women. Sticky summer nights. The biggest strip of night clubs ’tween Vegas and Miami Beach! Bossier City! One armed bandits! Teenage prostitutes! Drunken driving! All the things that make life worth living. One summer morning in 1967 Wayne said to me, “Roy, we can either get drunk here in Maynard or we can get drunk in Bossier City!” So we drove to Louisiana! And I mean, Ray, as soon as we got there, wham! Just like that things started to happen!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What happened?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: We saw a car wreck.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Wow!

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: That was nothin’. We saw three before we left town. We were in two of them.
(Pause.)

Wayne was a helluva driver. I tell you we started at one end of that Bossier Strip and worked our way to the other. Club Flamingo, the Log Cabin Club, Kim’s Lounge, and the immortal Merle Kimberly’s Whisk A-Go-Go. Ray, it had three dance floors that lit up!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Did you dance?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You know I don’t dance. Wayne danced.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Did you get in any fights?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: We got kicked out of The Ace’s Lounge and Mr. Torch for fighting.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You couldn’t get out of these fights.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Hell no. We started them. Then! At the Swamp Club, Wayne tried to pick up these two Italian girls. Well, their boyfriends didn’t like that one little bit. And let me tell you something, Ray. If you’re ever in that part of the world, don’t ever get involved with no Louisiana Dagos. There ain’t nothin’ worse than the Southern Mafia!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Are they the ones that pull the knife on you?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yeah, they’re the ones who—hey, you’ve heard this story before.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Drunken pause.)
You broke into my story. Where was I?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (With great enthusiasm.)

You were right at the part where the Italians pull out their knives, and you and Wayne run back to the truck to get your shotgun. But then the Italian guys pull out their guns and start shootin’ at you! But you make it back to the truck, and while Wayne backs the truck out of the parking lot you fire out the window at the Italians. Wayne backs up into one car, hits a fence, and then as he’s leaving the parking lot he side-swipes an oncoming Cadillac.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Annoyed.)
It was a Lincoln Continental.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Or a Lincoln Continental, depending on how drunk you are when you’re telling the story.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Indignant.)
That story don’t change! It happened. We had ourselves a time.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, sometimes you say a stray bullet kills an old woman.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Well, sometimes I forget that part.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: How can you forget gunning down an old woman?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: It wasn’t an old woman exactly. What it was . . . was a bowling alley. There was a bowling alley next to the parking lot.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: How could you mistake an old woman from a bowling alley?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: It was dark.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Oh.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Anyway, me and Wayne ended up in Kim’s Lounge. And Wayne begins to sweet talk this girl down at the end of the bar. And pretty soon he’s taking this girl out to the pickup truck. He told me it wouldn’t take long. So I ordered another drink. Then, in about five minutes old Wayne comes back in as white as a sheet and says: “Roy, let’s get the hell out of Bossier City.” So we did. But after only six hours on the Bossier Strip we had ourselves two fights, two car wrecks, had a gun battle with the Southern Mafia, and Wayne Wilder had french-kissed a man in a dress!
(Pause. Lifting beer.)
So Wayne, down in Huntsville—here’s to you boy.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (They both drink. Cletis Fullenroy enters L., dressed in J.C. Penney slacks, shirt, with a plastic pocket pencil holder and a pair of loafers.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Hey Ray. Hey Roy.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Hey Cletis.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: What you boys doin’ out here?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Ominously.)
We were just shooting it out with the Italian Mafia.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Shoot. There ain’t no Italians out here.
(Pause. He looks around.)
Are there?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You know any Italians, Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Can’t say as I do.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: That’s just as well. I have a feeling Italians would hate you on sight. Even little baby Italians without ever being told just how hideous you are would hate you.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Pause.)
Uh . . . yes.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: How are you Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Fine. Perfectly fine.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: That’s good.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: How are you, Ray?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I’m fine.
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You know, I’m part Italian myself. That’s right. My real mother was an Italian my father met during the war.
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Nervously.)
I see. So, what are you boys doing out here?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Nothing.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Nothing?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Nothing.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: How can you do nothing?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I can’t do nothing for very long. But Roy can do nothing night on to forever.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What you need out here, Skeeter Fullenroy?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Don’t call me that.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: That’s what I’ve always called you, Skeeter.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Yes, but I run an appliance store now.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What’s your full name, Skeeter?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Reluctantly.)
Cletis T. Fullenroy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Cletis T. Fullenroy. What does the “T” stand for?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Nothing. Don’t stand for nothing.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: It’s gotta stand for somethin’.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: No it don’t. My Daddy wanted me to have a middle initial. He’d never had one himself and he said it had killed his ambition.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Are you ambitious?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Someday I hope to go into politics.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You’d do and I’ll shoot you.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Smiling weakly.)
You’re a caution, Roy. You really are.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Why don’t you go away, Cletis?
(Pause.)
Tell you what. I’m going to close my eyes and think of pleasant thoughts like Christmas and reindeer and when I open them you’ll be gone. Like a bad dream.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy closes eyes and begins humming “Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer.” He opens eyes.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You ain’t gone, Cletis.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I know. I got something to talk to Ray about.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: It’s private.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Haven’t you figured out what to do in bed yet?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Flustered.)
What do you mean?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I hear you can’t find the vagina, Skeeter.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Who told you that? Did you tell him that?!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Now, Cletis…

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: You did! You did tell him that! I can’t believe you told him that!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It just slipped out.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: That’s my private sex life! Things like that just don’t slip out!

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Is that your problem, Skeeter? Have things been slipping out lately?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: No! And don’t call me Skeeter!

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What should I call you then, Skeeter?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I’m not talking to you.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Cletis—

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I’m not talking to you either.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Cletis, if you’re not talking to Roy and you’re not talking to me—

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Who the hell are you talking to, Skeeter?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Nobody and nothing.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Silence.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Sure are a lot of stars out tonight. Sure are. There’s the Big Dipper, and the Little Dipper, and there’s Orion.
(Pause.)
I haven’t seen stars like this since my honeymoon. Amy Lee and me, we camped out near Lake Cherokee up near Tulsa. I mean to tell you, that Oklahoma—that’s God’s country.
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (To Ray.)
I’m going in for a drink. Then I come back, you make sure this asshole’s gone.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy exits.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I don’t think Roy likes me.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: He hates you, Cletis.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Cletis takes out a pack of cigarettes. Begins to smoke.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: When did you start smokin’, Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: What time is it?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: About one.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I started about forty-five minutes ago.
(Miserably.)
I think I’m hooked.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: How do you like it so far?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: It’s all right. (Pause.) Tastes like shit though. Makes me dizzy too. (Pause.) And it makes your breath stink. Even your fingers stink.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Think you’ll stick with it?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I don’t think so. I think I’ll give it up. (Pause.) I don’t want to get lung cancer. (Pause.) In fact, I think I’ll give it up right now. (He drops cigarette and steps on it.) Whew. I feel better already. Now I can start clearing out my lungs. Tomorrow I’m going to start running track.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What made you take it up?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Severe depression. It was either cigarettes or alcohol. And you know what alcohol does to me. I could be a damn alcoholic if I didn’t watch it.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What’s your problem, Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Well…

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Is it about your wife?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: No damn it! It’s not about my wife! And by the way I resent the fact that you told Roy about my private sex life.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I apologize.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I had a few questions, that’s all! Just a few questions.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I apologize.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: That damn book. Shit. It showed a little squiggly line with an arrow that said “vagina.” How was I to know? A vagina don’t look nothin’ like a squiggly line.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No. But what does it look like?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: You know, I shouldn’t be talkin’ about this. I’m a Baptist and we just don’t sit around talkin’ about our wives’ sacred reproductive organs.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: OK.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: That’s all right. I forgive you. (Pause. Lost.) Ray. Why does Roy hate me?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: He doesn’t like your shoes.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Why?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: He just doesn’t like ’em.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Thoughtfully.) I’ve always looked up to Roy.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: So have I.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I don’t know. He was always like an idol to me, I suppose. He was real good in sports, and he was real popular with girls. And he had that 1959 pink Thunderbird convertible.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Still does.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Yeah. Then too, he was the only boy from Maynard to go off to Vit Nam.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I had football knee.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I had to run the appliance store.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What you drivin’ at, Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: See, it’s just that I always wanted to be just like Roy. See, I combed my hair like Roy. I bought cowboy shirts like Roy. I saw “Hud” six times. Nothin’ worked. Girls still didn’t like me. It ain’t easy when you got a name like Skeeter.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I know, Cletis.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: How do you know? How can you possibly know what it’s like to go through life as Skeeter? What did they call you growin’ up?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Ray.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Well with me it was either Skeeter or Cletis. Either way it was humiliation.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Get to the point.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I just always wanted to be like Roy. I had the hair combed right like him, had the shirts like him. Nothin’ worked. But deep down, deep down I know what was the difference between me and Roy.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What was that?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (With mystic awe.) A 1959 pink Thunderbird convertible.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Ah, I see.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: If I just had that car, life may have turned out different for me. I could see myself with Sandra Dee flyin’ along about 90 mph, the top down, Sandra’s long blonde hair wavin’ in the wind, telephone posts hummin’ by—whew there goes one! Whew, there goes one! It would have been a beautiful thing to behold. Life would have been complete. (Pause.) Well, Ray, tonight I had a chance to make my life complete. You can understand that can’t you, Ray?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I don’t know what the hell you’re talkin’ about, Cletis.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Cletis takes out a set of car keys from his pocket.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Here.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Pause.) These are the keys to Roy’s car.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Yes they are.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Where did you get these keys?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: You left ’em in on Angel’s Bar when you came back here lookin’ for Roy.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Roy loves that car, Cletis.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: It was a beautiful thing to behold.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Was? Was a beautiful thing to behold, Cletis? (Pause. Whisper.) What in the name of God have you done, Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I took it.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You took Roy’s 1959 pink Thunderbird convertible?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I did.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What did you do then?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I broke it.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You what?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I was into Angel’s Bar, I saw you lay the keys on the counter. You forgot you left ’em when you came out here.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: And you took ’em?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I just wanted to take a spin. I swear, just a spin.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What happened, Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I run it off the road.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Where, Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Down past ol’ man Purkey’s place at that curve in the road. I couldn’t hold it.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well that’s flat land there. There’s nothing to run into out there.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Sadly.) Think about it, Ray.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Pause.) There’s one tree.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: One gigantic, ancient, unmovable cottonwood tree.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You run the Thunderbird up against the cottonwood, didn’t you, Cletis?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I run the Thunderbird THROUGH the cottonwood, Ray.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Is it in one piece?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: The cottonwood is.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: And the Thunderbird?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: It hit that tree and fell apart like an old thing made of rust. There’s pieces of that car spread from here to yonder. The biggest piece burst into a great wad of fire. (Pause.) It was pretty.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Where were you?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I was thrown from the car, by a miracle. By all rights I should be dead.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Don’t worry. You will be.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: He’s really gonna be mad, ain’t he?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Mad? Hell, Cletis, he’ll kill you.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: That’s what I got to talk to you about.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I can’t do nothing to help you.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Help me? Help US! You’re my accomplice!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What d’you mean?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: You left the keys on the counter, didn’t you? It’s half your fault.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Pause.) That’s the way Roy’ll figure it too.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Will he? Good! Well, that’s what I want to talk to you about.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Why don’t you say you wrecked the car? You’re his brother! He won’t kill you! Now me, he hates me to begin with! But you — what’s the worst he could do?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Break my arms.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: That’s not so bad.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: And my legs too.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Now, his bark’s worse ’n his bite.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: His bite is pretty awful too.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Don’t tell him I did it! Please don’t tell him I did it. It’d be like throwing Christians to the lions. It’d be like the early Christian martyrs.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Cletis, the early Christian martyrs didn’t go around wrecking people’s cars!

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Almost in tears.) Please, Ray, please!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Shut up will you. I gotta think.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Think about what!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Shut up!

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: What’ll I do while you’re thinking?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Look at the stars.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I can’t look up at ’em, all I see is a dead Thunderbird.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Take up smokin’ again.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Cletis takes out cigarettes. Pause. He lights, shakes.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: (Talking to himself.) He can’t kill me. I’m a married man. That ought to count for something. (Pause.) Maybe if I move to Mexico. He might not follow me there. Or Central America! It’d be like Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid! I could go out in a blaze of glory shootin’ it out with the Federales! (Pause.) I wonder if you can take a U-Haul to Central America?
(He smokes.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Cletis. I been thinkin’.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: What you come up with?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Nothin’. There ain’t nothin’ to come up with.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Come on Ray. Get in the car. We can be in San Antone by dawn!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Forget it. You just get the hell out of here. I’ll take all the blame.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: What? You mean it? (Pumping his hand.) Goddamn that’s Christian of you goddamn. Now look Ray if you ever need anything from me anything at all. You just know where to find me. You come in to my Daddy’s store and I’ll get you a goddamn 10%—no 15% discount! I don’t care what Daddy says! You hear me?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I hear you, Cletis. Now get gone.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I just want to know how much I . . .

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy enters . . . bandage wrapped around right hand, singing, much drunker, a song like “Ruby.” Roy walks up to Cletis and begins dancing. They pull away.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: Thank you. You dance really fine, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What the hell you still doin’ here, Skeeter?

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I just wanted to say . . . (Cletis begins pumping Roy’s hand.) . . . that you’re one of the finest most respectful persons it’s ever been my privilege—

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Goddamnit, quit shakin’ my hand.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I just want to say that deep down, deep down I know you got a Christian respect for life, liberty, and love of family and friends.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: C’mon Skeeter. (Holding Cletis by lapels.) Let’s get one thing clear. I wouldn’t piss in your mouth if your guts was on fire.
(Releasing Cletis.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I know you wouldn’t. Thank you. Thank you. Well I’ll just be goin’ now.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Why don’t you do that little thing.

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I’ll just be goin’ now.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Don’t just say it... do it.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Cletis exits slowly L.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Look a here Ray. Tore my goddamn shirt pocket.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Cletis re-enters having heard this.)

Lines.Speaker: CLETIS
Lines.Line: I got a whole drawer full of shirts just like that one at home. You’re welcome to ’em. All of ’em.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Roar.) GIT!

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Cletis exits into L.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: How’d you tear your shirt . . .

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I was on my way out to the parkin’ lot. I wanted to see my car.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Did you see it?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Nah. I didn’t get to the parkin’ lot.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Good.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: No I got in a fight with Ricky Wright.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I’m surprised he’d fight with you.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: It took some doin’.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Why’d you fight him?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: He was drinkin’ Pearl Beer. It made me mad. Why would anybody drink that horsepiss Pearl when you could be drinkin’ Lone Star?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I wouldn’t drink it for money.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Want another?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Could do one.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I wished I could hear a coyote sometimes.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Me too. Ain’t none no more.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I know.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: We poisoned ’em all off.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Pause.) I’d sell my soul to hear a coyote.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy does a coyote howl. Ray does a half-hearted coyote howl.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I wouldn’t even mind a few drunken Indians now and then.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: We got rid a them fore we got even rid a they coyotes.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Goddamn Texas. You ain’t got coyotes; you ain’t got Indians. What the hell have you got Texas?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: We got International Harvester. We got plenty a them.
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Sometimes you wonder what this country musta been like in the early days. Goddamn when all this country was open and breathin’ free.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: There were Indians then.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Buffalo.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Buffalo chips.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: And there were . . . deer

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Uh-huh

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: And antelope

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: And before that

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Dinosaurs

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: And dinosaur chips.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: All sorts of ancient things. Hair of the dog?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No thank you.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Drinks.) Damn this stuff’ll kill you.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: That’s for damn sure.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Silence.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You know what I love. I love my wife. I love my country.
And I love my car. Whooo! I tell you. I’ve had myself some times in that car.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I’m glad you’ve got your memories, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I do. I got my memories. That car’s been some kind a pussy wagon. That there car’s my youth.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well we all have to grow up sometimes Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: In the spring of nineteen hundred and sixty one I took Edith Ellen Hyde out in that car a mine. Took her parkin’ out to Thompson’s road. That was the night I looked up her dress. Up until then I had no idea what life was all about.
(Pause.)
We kissed till we got half way good at it. Then she took off her shirt.
(Pause.)

That was the first nipple I’d had in my mouth since Mom’s. But nipples are like bicycles, once you learn you never forget how. Finally we got the windows all steamed up, and I couldn’t wait. Got the car to smell like the smell of a woman and I just had to see it. Edith Ellen didn’t want me to see it. Said it was bad enough me touchin’ it without wantin’ to look at it. She even tried to scare me. Said it looked God awful. But she couldn’t talk me out of it. I was a man with a mission. So I scooted over and scrunched down under the steerin’ column like this and she lifted up her skirt and I lit a match like that. And I looked at the damn thing.
(Pause.)

And y’know, y’wonder what the first explorer felt. The first explorer that climbed over that hill and saw—stretched out before him, in all its God glory—the grand canyon. Well that’s what I felt like when Edith Ellen Hyde lifted her skirt and said, “here it is,” I looked, and it was AWE INSPIRING. I felt like Adam. I felt like the man who discovered the Grand Canyon.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: That was like a sermon.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yes.
(Pause.)
You know, I been thinkin’ about that car lately, Ray.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Me too.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Do you realize that I’ve had more tail in that car than anybody else in this whole town?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, sure. You wouldn’t let no one else drive it but you.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I had myself the pleasures of my life in that back seat.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You used to let me smell the seats after you come home from a date. You used to say, “that’s the smell of a woman.” I appreciated it.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: It was nothin’. That car’s gonna be a museum piece in time. I’d like to pass on to my children and their children, somehow, a piece of that car.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You can give them each a piece Roy.
(Pause.)
Roy, I got somethin’ to tell you.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: All right.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It’s bad.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: All right.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It’s terrible.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Uh-huh.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It’s like an open wound.
(Pause.)
When you were in Vit Nam.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yes.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Me and Elizabeth.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You and Elizabeth what?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Me and Elizabeth…

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yes…

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Well, me and Elizabeth…

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I’ve heard this part, Ray.
(Pause.)
Stop playin’ with that tractor gasket.
(Pause. Slowly.)
Ray—

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Made love.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You and Elizabeth

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Elizabeth and me

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: It’s the same thing

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yes

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: While I was in Vit Nam?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Yes.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: Pause. Roy looks at Ray. Ray looks at the ground.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Damn. Shit damn.
(Pause.)
What have you got to say for yourself?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I’m sorry.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Is that all?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I’m real sorry.

Lines.Speaker: ROY. (Standing, pacing.)
Lines.Line: Well, shit. I just can’t believe it.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Roy—

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Shut your mouth.
Pause.

My own little brother. I can’t believe it. I taught you your life. I taught you how to swim, how to drive a car, how to pass a football, I taught you how to jack off. The most important things in life, I gave to you. And this is my reward?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: What can I say?

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Not a damn thing. I can’t believe this is happening to me. My own goddamn little brother and my own goddamn wife. You know that’s against the Ten Commandments, don’t you? You’ve just broken one of God’s biggest laws, that’s all. You have coveted your own brother’s wife.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: What does coveted mean?

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: It means fuck, ya damn fool. You have fucked your own brother’s wife. You know what that’s called?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: No, what?

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Sodomy.

Lines.Speaker: RAY. (With terror.)
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: That’s right, Ray. Sodomy. Boy, I’d hate to be in your shoes. It says it right there in the Bible:
“Thou had better not commit adultery, nor fuck thy brother’s wife, nor covet thou his sheep, nor covet thou his ox…”

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I wouldn’t fuck my brother’s ox.

Lines.Speaker: ROY. (Groaning.)
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I wouldn’t even fuck my own ox.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: That’s not what it means.

Lines.Speaker: ROY. (Groaning.)
Lines.Line: No—

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I wouldn’t even fuck my own ox.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: That’s not what it means.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: You said “covet” meant fuck.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Sometimes it does, sometimes it doesn’t. See what I mean, Ray? See what I’m drivin’ at?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: I’m talkin’ about the Bible, boy. I’m talkin’ about the goddamn Garden of Eden.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You are?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Of course. In the Garden of Eden, God made Adam. And he made animals. And He looked down and he saw that it was good. Then He looked down and saw Adam was alone and he saw that that was perverted.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Why was it?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Because Adam was alone. He had nothing to occupy his mind. He was walking around perverting the Garden of Eden.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What was he doing?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Figure it out, Ray. The guy was alone and he was horny.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Oh. Then what?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Then God made Eve, and the bitch screwed the goose and they all got kicked out on their ass. Get my point?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: God punished them. They broke some of his major laws just like you and he punished them.
(Pause.)
When did you and Elizabeth fornicate on me?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Uh… about two years ago…

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: When two years ago?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Winter… no the air conditioner was on the first time—

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: The first time! Was there more than once?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Huh?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: More than once? How many times? Did you do it a lot?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No, not a lot.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You better tell me the truth. How many times?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I’m not good at countin’.
(A horrible silence.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Where?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: In your bed.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: In my bed! Is that all?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Once on the floor.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: The floor!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Once in the bathroom.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Once on the kitchen table.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: The kitchen table. I’ll never eat off it again. You goddamn degenerate. I swear. This is the most perverted thing that ever happened to me. My whole house defiled. Guess I’ll just have to pitch a tent in the back yard.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Once in the back yard.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray still seated. Roy stalks around with contained rage.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I wonder what I’m going to do with you, Ray?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I don’t know.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: I think what I ought to do…

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy picks up a two-by-four near the shed.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: …is take this here two-by-four upside your skull.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Don’t do that, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Why the hell not?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: It might kill me.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: You ought to be killed.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Don’t say that to me. I’m your little brother.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: I oughta smash your skull in.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: War has made you bloodthirsty, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: That’s what it’s supposed to do, you damn fool!

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: But if you killed me—

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: What?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I’d die.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: So?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: If I die… I won’t know what to do.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy throws away the lumber. Ray braces for the blow he feels sure is coming. Roy walks to Ray slowly. Stands over him. Roy snatches Ray’s cap and beats Ray furiously with it. His anger subsiding, Roy walks over to the side of the bar, leans against it, moaning like an animal in pain.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: The thing is… nothing has been the same since I come back. Things I see… people I see… it’s like they never was. The thing is… I can’t seem to get nothing started no more… ’cause… ’cause… see, me and Elizabeth, we had it good once. And I never thought she would do me like… I mean I know I’m a hard man to live with… but… she’s a wonderful woman… a wonderful woman… see, the thing is… goddammit all to hell.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy comes back to where Ray is sitting. Gives him his hat.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Are you gonna hit me now, Roy?

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Please. It’ll make you feel better.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Wish you would. You’ll feel better. I promise.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: All right.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy walks in front of where Ray is sitting. Without breaking stride, he suddenly turns and belts Ray in the mouth. Ray flies off the car seat he is sitting on. Roy goes back to the side of the bar.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: You said you weren’t going to hit me.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Changed my mind.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Do you feel any better?

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: No.
(Pause.)

You see, me and Elizabeth—we were real close once. It’s like she trusted me… she knew I had my faults when she married me… but deep down we always loved each other. And I’ll always love her, no matter what.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: That’s good, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: No matter what comes between us, I’ll always love her.
(Quietly.)
See, we were together. And we’ll always be together.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy crosses to bench. Sits.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: But now I got this hurt that I’m carrying around with me.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I understand.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: No you don’t. How could you understand? Ain’t nobody can understand my hurt. It’s deep down. No one understands. Who could possibly understand my hurt?

Lines.Speaker: RAY. (Pause.)
Lines.Line: Hank Williams.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Shut up.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray crosses to bench. Sits. Roy begins drinking from a pint bottle of whiskey. He shares some with his brother. They sit in silence. From Angel’s bar drifts the sound of a song like “Your Cheatin’ Heart.” Ray begins to sing along softly. Roy joins in. They finish the first verse and together bow their heads. There is a short pause, then in unison, with extra punch, they begin singing the second verse and finish the song with gusto. They then fall silent.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: You still mad at me?

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: You gonna hit on me anymore?

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: No. You’re my brother.
(Pause.)
Besides, you can’t help it if you got brain damage.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: I ain’t got brain damage.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: We been keeping it from you. Mom and me.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: You made that up. I ain’t got no brain damage.
(Pause.)
I wonder why in hell she picked you?

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: ’Cause I was your brother. I’ve wanted to tell you for a long time. But everything has just fucked up tonight. I wanted to get it all over with at once.
(Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: Lotta things happen.

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: ROY.
Lines.Line: You know what we’re gonna do about all this?

Lines.Speaker: RAY.
Lines.Line: What?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Nothing. We’re not gonna tell her you told me.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: OK.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Lotta things change. But things are gonna stay the same around here.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: OK.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (They spit in their palms and shake on it. As an afterthought Ray wipes his hands. Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Roy?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Huh?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I don’t got brain damage like you said. Do I?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: ’Course not. You’re my brother ain’t you?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Pause. They look at each other, hug then break away embarrassed.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Well, enough of this shit. Let’s go home.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: We can’t.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Why not?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Your car ain’t here.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: You said it was.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I thought it was.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Pause.)
Where is it?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Sit down, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Sits.)
Where is it?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Down past old man Purkey’s out in a field.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: What’s it doin’ out there?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It’s wrecked.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy takes a long sip of whiskey.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: My pink 1959 Thunderbird convertible?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: She’s gone, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: How did it happen?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Run off the road into a tree.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: That old cottonwood tree?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Can you fix her?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Was it quick?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: She burnt all up. ’Cept for the parts that don’t burn.
(Pause.)
You all right, Roy?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Sure. Don’t worry about it. I can handle it. No big deal. Worse things have happened in the history of man. I can’t think of what . . .
(Nearly sobbing.)
My car, my car . . . why’d you do it Ray, was it out of spite? Wasn’t Elizabeth enough for you, you pig!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It wasn’t me.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Who then? (Pause.) You were here with me all night. You took Elizabeth home, we played Vit Nam, we did the Bossier City story, then . . . then . . . then CLETS!!

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Roy!

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I’m gonna kill him. I may get the chair, but I’m gonna kill him.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: (Pause.) I’m gonna torture him first and then I’m gonna kill him. I may get the chair, but Im gonna kill him. I’m gonna torture him first and then I’m gonna -  I Know! (Pause.) I’m gonna castrate him first and then I’m gonna—I know! I’ll strap him to the rear bumper and drag the sonofabitch . . . wait a minute . . . I don’t even have a rear bumper no more. Can I borrow your pickup?

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I’m using it tomorrow.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Never mind. Forget it. There’s something I’ve gotta do first.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Before you kill Clets?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Before I do anything.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: What you gotta do?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I gotta puke.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy runs, staggers off R.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Starlight, starbright, First star I seen tonight,I wished I may, I wished I might, Have the wish I wished tonight.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (We hear Ray vomit offstage. Pause. Roy returns, pale and shaken.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You OK?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You puke?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yeah.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You want anything?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Gimme a Star.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Ray hands him a Lone Star beer.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: It all came up?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Yep. It all came up. The beer, Clets, Elizabeth, Vit Nam, my car, you, me.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Breathe some of this night air, you’ll feel better. (Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I guess my car’s scattered all over the field.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Clets said it exploded on impact.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: I bet it was a beautiful sight to behold.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I bet it was too. (Silence.)

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Guess I’ll have to walk home.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: I’ll walk you home, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: OK.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: We can follow the creek bed yonder.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: OK.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: We got plenty of moon and stars. ’Member what the old man used to say about stars? “The light we seen tonight left those stars millions and millions of years ago. Some of those stars could be dead by now. They all could be dead. We might be all alone but we’re still seein’ ’em shine.”

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Pause. Ray hands Roy his car keys.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: You can get a new car.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Won’t be the same.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Things is never the same.

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (The brothers look at each other.)

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: No. (Pause.)
I’m going down to Minden tomorrow. Want to come?

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: No.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: (Pause.) You got to look out for the silver lining, Roy.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Ain’t none.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: Always is. Mom says.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Mom’s senile.

Lines.Speaker: RAY
Lines.Line: There’s always something to be thankful for.

Lines.Speaker: ROY
Lines.Line: Well (defiantly) . . . at least I ain’t in Oklahoma!

Lines.Speaker: SD
Lines.Line: (Roy throws his arm around Ray, gives a yell, and they exit.)

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: How things are with her.
What do you mean by that?

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Dr. Rush asked that I speak to you personally, Mr. Girard,
to make known to you certain surprising developments that have
only now come to our attention regarding the state or condition of your wife, sir.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: I do not know, sir, if you value your time, but I do mine.
Please be brief.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: I will try.
May I sit, sir?

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: By all means, sit or do whatever you wish, only let us get this bothersome business settled.
If there is some question as to the adequacy of the monthly payment of her expenses I shall naturally
increase the allotment to whatever sum is necessary,
although I will remind you that both you and Dr. Hutchinson assured me the amount settled on was more than sufficient.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Oh, no, Mr, Girard.
Let me assure you that it is not a question of costs.
The generosity of your settlement is well-known by all concerned in this matter.
No, the hospital could not wish for a more beneficial arrangement.
No sir, it is not a financial matter that brings me here but rather one concerning your wife's mental and... physical condition.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: As I am quite familiar with the one and not at all interested in the other,
I doubt that anything you have to tell me could be of the least interest to me.
Nonetheless, as she yet remains my wife, and as you have taken it upon yourself to interrupt my affairs with this matter,
I am willing to listen to you, only briefly.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: I have rather surprising news for you and for all of us at the hospital.
Mrs. Girard is with child.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Yes.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Do you mean, sir, you have been cognizant of that fact?

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: I have.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: And were you so aware when you had her brought to us?

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Yes, sir, I was.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: And yet you brought her to us to be confined with the mentally insane?

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Most certainly.
I find it curious that I need point out to you that physical conditions have
nothing whatsoever to do with the state of one's mind.
I brought my wife to you because she is insane.
That has nothing to do with whatever physical condition she may be in.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Mr. Girard, I am happy to tell you that is not always the case.
Although it is true that the mind functions separately from the body which supports it,
there is one major exception to that general rule.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: And what is that?

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: The state of pregnancy.
It is not uncommon for a woman with child to exhibit many of the signs we ordinarilly ascribe only to the insane.
You see, many of the irregularities you spoke of are quite possibly
due to nothing more than the naturally flighty state of mind brought on in many women
by the physical changes in the female body during the pre-natal period.
When a woman is with child she is wont to have imaginings,
to suffer strange cravings,
certain outbursts of temper and language that one does not expect.
In other words, her behavior may become erratic in a fashion that is often alarming
to those who are used to a more sedate and stately behavior on the part of the lady.
Consequently, under these circumstances, the board of directors and I agree
that it is in everyone's best interest for you to remove Mrs. Girard from our care,
allow her the confinement usually recommended for the later stages of a pregnancy, and,
after her deliverance of your child, she will most probably prove to be as sane as she ever was.
What I am trying to tell you, sir, is the happy news that Mrs. Girard is quite possibly, even probably, as sane as you or I. (Pause.)

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: My wife, sir, is insane.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Mr. Girard...

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: (Interrupting.) I said, she is insane.
(Slight pause.)

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Your wife suffers from severe headaches.
She is extremely nervous.
Upon occasion she has exhibited erratic behavior,
and outbursts of abusive language.
If she were not pregnant, there might be cause to think an extended rest,
close care, and quiet might not be enough to restore her normal faculties.
However, as she is with child...

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: That is a fact I wish you and the members of the board to disregard.
Whether she is, as you say, with child or not need be no concern of yours.
Nor of anyone else's.
I tell you in all candor it is not a fact I wish ever be known.
Nor do I feel kindly disposed toward you, sir, for your having pressed this loathsome fact to my consideration.
True, I was previously aware of... her condition.
But I have chosen of my own free will to ignore it.
It is my wish, sir, that you and all the members of the board do likewise.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Sir, I am amazed.
I have just told you that your wife is soon to be delivered of your child,
a child you seem determined will be gestated and born in a madhouse,
amidst scenes of the most horrid chaos,
amongst madmen and madwomen,
and you advise me to ignore it?

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Precisely.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Mr. Girard, I am trying to tell you that
given these newly discovered circumstances we cannot permit you
to leave Mrs. Girard in these conditions.
Indeed, to place such woman in such surroundings at such a time
is to pose the greatest possible threat to her sanity,
no matter how sound of mind she might have been beforehand.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: I see. (He writes out a draft.)
Perhaps this will be suficient. (He hands the check out to MR. PHILLIPS, but PHILLIPS does not take it.)

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Mr. Girard.
It is not a question of money, sir.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Is that fact?

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: It is, sir.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Come, come, sir.
I am a loyal and an active supporter of our new republic, and I am a businessman.
In both capacities it has been my experience
that everything in life is a question of money.
I have found no friendship, no love, no loyalty, and no fact
that cannot be altered by large enough draft or a tidy enough sum of money.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Surely you can't believe that, Mr. Girard.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Believe it? I know it.
It is fact, sir. I have staked my career on it,
and, as you can see, I have prospered.
(MR. GIRARD thrusting the check at him.) Look at this!

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: (Taking the check.) Three thousand dollars.
But this is made out...

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: To you, sir.
That is payment in advance which I hope you will be gracious enough to accept from me.
You see, I do not choose to be concerned any further with any
diffculties that may arise due to my wife's... confinement.
Therefore, I humbly ask that you personally see to it that
whatever needs to be done is done to keep all references to her,
her child, if it should live, and any other dificulties concerning her, beyond my awareness.
As to the board of trustees, tell them I will assume all responsibility for this matter,
in writing, of course.
Tell them, also, that within a month of my unfortunate wife's demise
the hospital shall receive a check identical to the one you hold now.
You need not ever mention this check.
I feel a man's finances are no one's affair but his own.
Tell them, also, that upon my death, I will bequeath
Pennsylvania Hospital no less a sum than ten times the amount written there.
All this, of course, to be above and beyond the sums already agreed to for her daily care,
and given in deepest gratitude for the superb treatment accorded the insane woman who unfortunately still bears my name.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: I don't know what to say.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Answer only this: can it be done?

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Yes. (Lights.)

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Answer only this: can it be done?

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Yes.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Good! See to it then. I believe you know your way out?

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: But, sir...

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: Sir?

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: What of the child?

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: That, sir, is no concern of mine.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: I would not have a child of mine born under such conditions for all the money in the world.

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: And I can assure you, sir, neither would I.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Mr. Girard. Do you mean the child...

Lines.Speaker: Girard
Lines.Line: I bid you good day, sir.

Lines.Speaker: Phillips
Lines.Line: Sir. (Lights fade on GIRARD and PHILLIPS, FURIES set MARY free.)

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: Bring him!
(GIRARD ENTERS. He does not know where is he for a moment. The FURIES surround him and then hide behind him.)

Lines.Speaker: GIRARD.
Lines.Line: How did you get here?

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: I am where you placed me. Stephen.

Lines.Speaker: GIRARD.
Lines.Line: Where I... I shouldn't be in this place.

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: (Laughing.) No, Stephen?

Lines.Speaker: GIRARD.
Lines.Line: No, I...

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: (Laughing.) Do you think I'm a vision?

Lines.Speaker: GIRARD.
Lines.Line: No. Of course not. I... no.
(MRS. HATCHER ENTERS with MR. PHILLIPS.)

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: Is it true as they say that the baby is that of Mary Girard's?

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: The baby's origin is a matter of no importance, Mrs. Hatcher.
You and Mr. Hatcher will receive the set sum for nursing the child
just as you have with all the other children you have cared for in the past.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: Yes, Mr. Phillips. I do appreciate you're calling on me again.
My health is ever so much better now than it used to be.

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: The past is past, Mrs. Hatcher.

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: Why have you done this to me?

Lines.Speaker: GIRARD.
Lines.Line: I had no choice. You are insane.

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: Don't lie to me, Stephen.
Even if I were insane you could have spared me this.
Both you and I know what my behavior was.
You know as well as I that I am sane.

Lines.Speaker: GIRARD.
Lines.Line: I hope that is true.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: Oh. Such a pretty little baby.
And a girl.
What is her name?

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: Her name is Mary!

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: Call her whatever you wish. It is immaterial.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: Oh, I shall take good care of this one, Mr. Phillips.
There won't be no repeats of my old mistakes.
I'm ever so much better now.

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: I told you not to speak of the past, Mrs. Hatcher.
Both for your sake and for the sake of the Hospital
it is best if you keep hidden from your neighbors the source from which you've received this child.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: (Exiting.) Oh, I shall love having her.
I am truly grateful, I've never had a little girl before.
I think I'll call you... Rose.
Rose is my name, you see, and you will be my little girl.

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: (Calling after her.) Mrs. Hatcher!

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: She told me you hated me like the devil.
I didn't believe her until this moment.

Lines.Speaker: GIRARD.
Lines.Line: Who told you that?

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: (Calling.) Mrs. Hatcher!

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: (Returning.) Yes, Mr. Phillips?

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: Mrs. Hatcher.
I would caution you against becoming too fond of this child.
It is, after all, a child born under unpropitious circumstances.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: What do you mean, Mr. Phillips?

Lines.Speaker: NIR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: I am asking you to treat it the same as you would any other child.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: Oh, I will sir.

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: Yes? Well, good.
Yes, I would have you treat it just though it were... a normal child.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: Isn't it a normal child, Mr. Phillips?

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: Oh, I didn't mean to alarm you, Mrs. Hatcher.
This baby seems normal, doesn't it?
It needs all the care and attention other children require.
Only there is a difference.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: What difference, Mr. Phillips? Tell me, please.

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: Don't let him do this!

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: It's mother is a patient among the insane.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: Oh no!

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: The baby has been born by and among those possessed of devils.
(The FURIES dance.)

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: God preserve us!

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: Born by and among those possessed of devils.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: God preserve us.

Lines.Speaker: MR. PHILLIPS.
Lines.Line: By and among those possessed of devils.

Lines.Speaker: MRS. HATCHER.
Lines.Line: God preserve us. (MRS. HATCHER and MR. PHILLIPS EXIT.)

Lines.Speaker: MARY.
Lines.Line: Is that what I am to you?
When we met I was sixteen,
a servant carrying water through the streets.
I laughed at everything then.
And I was happy without even knowing it.
